lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 Living and non-living things — Core teaching
learning_objectives: Identify many different kinds of living things.
Explain that living plants and animals carry out the seven life processes: feeding, growing, reproducing, breathing, excreting, sensing and moving.
Recognise that some things that appear not to be living can carry on living under the right conditions.
lessons.lesson_body:
In this lesson learners study living and non-living things using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Identify many different kinds of living things. Key vocabulary: Living, non-living, things. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 21 ---
NATURAL SCIENCES AND TECHNOLOGY GRADES 4-6
16
CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS)
SECTION 3: CONTENT TABLES
natural sCienCes and teCHnoloGy: Grade 4
Grade 4 term 1
strands:    natural  sCien Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on living and non-living things, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says living and non-living things happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Living And Non-Living Things requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about living and non-living things is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on living and non-living things?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying living and non-living things?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When germs not recognised as living appears, return to the worked example for living and non-living things and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about living and non-living things and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.1.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect living and non-living things to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for living and non-living things. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: germs not recognised as living, dormant seeds classified as non living. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about living and non-living things without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says habitats of animals happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Habitats Of Animals requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about habitats of animals is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: A hypothesis cannot be tested.
options.D: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on habitats of animals?

options.A: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.B: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying habitats of animals?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When habitat seen as only a house appears, return to the worked example for habitats of animals and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about habitats of animals and teach it to a partner using the story approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.1.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect habitats of animals to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for habitats of animals. Use the story variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: habitat seen as only a house, animals assumed to survive in any habitat. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about habitats of animals without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on solid materials, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?

step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results

options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says strong frame structures happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Strong Frame Structures requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about strong frame structures is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: A hypothesis cannot be tested.
options.D: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on strong frame structures?

options.A: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.B: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying strong frame structures?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When indigenous structures seen as not engineered appears, return to the worked example for strong frame structures and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about strong frame structures and teach it to a partner using the story approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.2.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect strong frame structures to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for strong frame structures. Use the story variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: square frames assumed as stable as triangles, struts confused with solid walls. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about strong frame structures without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying energy around us?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When input and output energy swapped appears, return to the worked example for energy around us and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about energy around us and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.3.2 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect energy around us to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for energy around us. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: input and output energy swapped, stored energy not recognised until used. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about energy around us without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on energy around us, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says energy around us happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Energy Around Us requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about energy around us is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 4.NST.3.3 covers Movement and energy in a system. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.
lessons.explanation: Learners practise movement and energy in a system using concise exam-style prompts and answer checking. CAPS focus: Explain that musical instruments are systems using movement input energy. Key vocabulary: Movement, energy, system. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 4.NST.3.3 covers Movement and energy in a system. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on movement and energy in a system, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says movement and energy in a system happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Movement And Energy In A System requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about movement and energy in a system is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on movement and energy in a system?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: Any object chosen at random
options.D: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying movement and energy in a system?

options.A: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.B: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When instrument system parts not connected to output appears, return to the worked example for movement and energy in a system and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about movement and energy in a system and teach it to a partner using the exam style approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.3.3 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect movement and energy in a system to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for movement and energy in a system. Use the exam style variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sound seen as not energy, instrument system parts not connected to output. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about movement and energy in a system without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 4.NST.3.4 covers Energy and sound. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on energy and sound, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says energy and sound happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Energy And Sound requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about energy and sound is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on energy and sound?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying energy and sound?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When sound travels only through air appears, return to the worked example for energy and sound and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about energy and sound and teach it to a partner using the real world sa approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.3.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect energy and sound to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for energy and sound. Use the real world sa variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sound travels only through air, vibration not linked to sound source. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about energy and sound without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 Planet Earth — Guided steps
learning_objectives: Describe Earth as a sphere made of rock.
Identify land, water and air as main surface features of Earth.
Recognise continents, islands, oceans, seas, air and habitats as features of Earth.
lessons.lesson_body:
Learners move through planet earth one guided step at a time with teacher checkpoints. CAPS focus: Describe Earth as a sphere made of rock. Key vocabulary: Planet, Earth, NATURAL, SCIENCES. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 15 ---

CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS)
2.4 orGanisation oF  tHe natural sCienCes and teCHnoloGy CurriCulum 
In this curriculum, Grades 4 to 6 Natur Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on planet earth, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says planet earth happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Planet Earth requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about planet earth is supported by observation?

options.A: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.B: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on planet earth?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying planet earth?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When stars seen as tiny nearby lights appears, return to the worked example for planet earth and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about planet earth and teach it to a partner using the step by step approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect planet earth to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for planet earth. Use the step by step variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: earth seen as flat from local view, air not seen as part of earth system. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about planet earth without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

learning_objectives: Describe the Sun as a star made of hot gas.
Explain that the Sun gives out heat and light.
Explain that the Sun is much bigger than Earth, very far away, and the closest star to Earth.
lessons.lesson_body:
This visual lesson helps learners see the sun with diagrams, models and labelled drawings. CAPS focus: Describe the Sun as a star made of hot gas. Key vocabulary: NATURAL, SCIENCES, TECHNOLOGY, GRADES. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 19 ---

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the sun, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the sun happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Sun requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the sun is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the sun?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: Any object chosen at random
options.D: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the sun?

options.A: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.B: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When closest star confused with nearby object appears, return to the worked example for the sun and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about the sun and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.2 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the sun to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the sun. Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sun seen as a planet, closest star confused with nearby object. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the sun without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 The Sun — Visual models
learning_objectives: Describe the Sun as a star made of hot gas.
Explain that the Sun gives out heat and light.
Explain that the Sun is much bigger than Earth, very far away, and the closest star to Earth.
lessons.lesson_body:
This visual lesson helps learners see the sun with diagrams, models and labelled drawings. CAPS focus: Describe the Sun as a star made of hot gas. Key vocabulary: NATURAL, SCIENCES, TECHNOLOGY, GRADES. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 19 ---

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the sun, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the sun happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Sun requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the sun is supported by observation?

options.A: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.B: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the sun?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the sun?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When closest star confused with nearby object appears, return to the worked example for the sun and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about the sun and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.2 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the sun to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the sun. Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sun seen as a planet, closest star confused with nearby object. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the sun without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 The Earth and the Sun — Core teaching
learning_objectives: Explain that Earth moves around the Sun in an orbit.
Recognise the Sun as a star at the centre of the solar system.
Recognise Earth as one of eight planets in the solar system.
lessons.lesson_body: In this lesson learners study the earth and the sun using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain that Earth moves around the Sun in an orbit. Key vocabulary: Earth, reference, covers, Explain. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 4.NST.4.3 covers The Earth and the Sun. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.
lessons.explanation: In this lesson learners study the earth and the sun using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain that Earth moves around the Sun in an orbit. Key vocabulary: Earth, reference, covers, Explain. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 4.NST.4.3 covers The Earth and the Sun. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the earth and the sun, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the earth and the sun happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Earth And The Sun requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the earth and the sun is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the earth and the sun?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the earth and the sun?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When sun orbits earth appears, return to the worked example for the earth and the sun and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about the earth and the sun and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.3 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the earth and the sun to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the earth and the sun. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sun orbits earth, pluto counted as one of eight planets. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the earth and the sun without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the earth and the sun. Use the exam style variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: sun orbits earth, pluto counted as one of eight planets. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the earth and the sun without looking at the notes. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the earth and the sun, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the earth and the sun happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Earth And The Sun requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the earth and the sun is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: A hypothesis cannot be tested.
options.D: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the earth and the sun?

options.A: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.B: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the earth and the sun?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When sun orbits earth appears, return to the worked example for the earth and the sun and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

learning_objectives: Describe the Moon as a ball of rock in space with no air or water.
Compare the Moon with Earth and the Sun by size and distance.
Explain that phases of the Moon result from the changing pattern of sunlight visible on the Moon.
lessons.lesson_body:
Learners connect the moon to everyday South African contexts such as shops, sport and community tasks. CAPS focus: Describe the Moon as a ball of rock in space with no air or water. Key vocabulary: Moon, NATURAL, SCIENCES, TECHNOLOGY. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 19 ---

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the moon, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the moon happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Moon requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the moon is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the moon?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the moon?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When moon makes its own light appears, return to the worked example for the moon and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about the moon and teach it to a partner using the real world sa approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the moon to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the moon. Use the real world sa variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: moon makes its own light, moon phase changes caused by earth shadow each night. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the moon without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.

lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 Rocket systems — Core teaching
learning_objectives: Explain that people have used rockets to go into space and travel to the Moon.
Describe a rocket as a system used to propel vehicles into space.
Explain that a rocket moves by pushing exhaust gases out through its back end.
lessons.lesson_body:
In this lesson learners study rocket systems using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain that people have used rockets to go into space and travel to the Moon. lessons.subject: Natural Sciences and Technology
lessons.title: Grade 4 Rocket systems — Story context
learning_objectives: Explain that people have used rockets to go into space and travel to the Moon.
Describe a rocket as a system used to propel vehicles into space.
Explain that a rocket moves by pushing exhaust gases out through its back end.
lessons.lesson_body:
A short South African classroom story introduces rocket systems before learners practise the skill. CAPS focus: Explain that people have used rockets to go into space and travel to the Moon. Key vocabulary: Rocket, systems, NATURAL, SCIENCES. Observe the example, name the process, and use evidence from the scenario to justify each answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 20 ---

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on rocket systems, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says rocket systems happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Rocket Systems requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about rocket systems is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: A hypothesis cannot be tested.
options.D: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on rocket systems?

options.A: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.B: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying rocket systems?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When rocket moves because air pushes from front appears, return to the worked example for rocket systems and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 4 example about rocket systems and teach it to a partner using the story approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 4.NST.4.5 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect rocket systems to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for rocket systems. Use the story variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: rocket moves because air pushes from front, model results not linked to system variables. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: natural sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about rocket systems without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports natural sciences learning at home.
